definition: A snapshot of your code at a specific point in time. Think of it like a save point in a video game — you can always go back to it.
example: After finishing a feature, run `git commit -m "Add login page"` to save that version.

definition: A holding zone where you pick which changes to include in your next commit. Like putting items in a shopping cart before checkout.
example: `git add login.js` puts just that one file in the staging area.

definition: A separate line of development. Like creating a copy of your project to try something new without affecting the original.
example: `git checkout -b feature/login` creates a new branch called 'feature/login'.

definition: Combining changes from one branch into another. Like taking your experimental work and adding it back to the main project.
example: `git merge feature/login` brings the login branch changes into your current branch.

definition: When two people changed the same lines of code differently. Git can't decide which version to keep, so it asks you to choose.
example: Git shows <<<<<<< and >>>>>>> markers around conflicting code. You delete the markers and keep the right version.

definition: A folder that Git is tracking. Contains your code and its entire version history.
example: Your project folder becomes a repo when you run `git init` inside it.

definition: A version of your repository stored on the internet (like GitHub). Lets you share code and back it up.
example: `git push origin main` sends your code to the remote repository on GitHub.

definition: A request to merge your changes into the main codebase. Other developers can review your code before it's merged.
example: On GitHub, you create a PR to ask your team to review and merge your feature branch.

definition: A list of function calls that led to an error, shown bottom-to-top. It tells you WHERE the error happened and HOW the code got there.
example:
TypeError: Cannot read property 'name' of undefined
    at getUser (user.js:15)
    at handleLogin (auth.js:42)

definition: Text that tells you what went wrong in your code. Always read this FIRST before trying to fix anything.
example: 'TypeError: x is not a function' means you're trying to call something as a function that isn't one.

definition: A tool in your browser (or terminal) that shows messages, errors, and lets you run code for testing. Your #1 debugging tool.
example: Press F12 in your browser, click 'Console' tab, and you'll see any errors your code produced.

definition: A command that prints a value to the console. The simplest debugging technique — just print stuff to see what's happening.
example: `console.log('user:', user)` will show you what the user variable contains.

definition: An attack where someone injects malicious code into a website that other users then run. Like someone slipping a note into a book that tricks the next reader.
example: If a comment box doesn't sanitize input, an attacker could type `<script>steal(cookies)</script>` and it would run in other users' browsers.

definition: An attack where someone puts database commands into an input field to manipulate your database. Like telling a robot butler 'give me the key AND open the safe'.
example: Typing `' OR 1=1 --` into a login form could bypass authentication if the app doesn't use parameterized queries.

definition: A secret password that identifies your app when talking to an external service. NEVER put this in your frontend code or share it publicly.
example: Your Stripe API key should go in a `.env` file, never in JavaScript that runs in the browser.

definition: A way for two pieces of software to talk to each other. Like a waiter taking your order (request) to the kitchen (server) and bringing back food (response).
example: When you load weather data in your app, you're calling a weather API that returns JSON data.

definition: A specific URL that an API listens on for requests. Each endpoint does one thing.
example: `GET /api/users` is an endpoint that returns a list of users. `POST /api/users` creates a new user.

definition: The part of an app that users see and interact with. Runs in the browser. Built with HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.
example: The login form you see, the buttons you click, the animations — that's all frontend.

definition: The part of an app that runs on the server. Handles data, business logic, and talks to the database. Users never see this directly.
example: When you click 'Log in', the backend checks your password against the database and decides if you get access.

definition: An organized collection of data that your app reads from and writes to. Like a giant spreadsheet that your code can query.
example: PostgreSQL, MongoDB, and SQLite are all databases. Your user accounts, posts, and settings are stored in one.

definition: The structure of your database — what tables exist, what columns each has, and how they relate. Like a blueprint for your data.
example: A users table might have columns: id, email, password_hash, created_at.

definition: A script that changes your database structure (add a table, rename a column). Like version control for your database.
example: A migration file might say: 'CREATE TABLE posts (id, title, body, user_id, created_at)'.

definition: A setting stored outside your code that changes based on where it runs (dev, staging, production). Keeps secrets out of your codebase.
example: DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@host:5432/mydb — stored in a .env file, never committed to git.

definition: Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment. Automated processes that test your code and deploy it when you push changes.
example: When you push to GitHub, CI runs your tests automatically. If they pass, CD deploys your app to production.

definition: The process of putting your app on the internet so real users can access it. Going from 'works on my machine' to 'works for everyone'.
example: Running `vercel deploy` takes your Next.js app and puts it on a server with a public URL.

definition: A test that checks if one small piece of code (a function, a component) works correctly in isolation.
example: Testing that your `calculateTotal(items)` function returns the right number for a given list of items.

definition: A test that checks if multiple pieces of your app work together correctly (API + database, frontend + backend).
example: Testing that creating a user via the API actually saves them to the database and returns the right response.

definition: A performance bug where your code makes 1 query to get a list, then N more queries to get details for each item. Should be 1-2 queries total.
example: Loading 100 blog posts, then running a separate query for each post's author = 101 queries instead of 2.

definition: Storing a copy of data so you don't have to fetch/compute it again. Like writing down a phone number instead of looking it up every time.
example: Storing API responses in memory for 5 minutes so repeat requests are instant instead of hitting the database.

definition: Organizing code so each file/module handles one thing. The UI code shouldn't contain database logic, and vice versa.
example: Keep your API route handlers separate from your database queries, and both separate from your UI components.

definition: Restructuring existing code without changing what it does. Making it cleaner, faster, or easier to understand.
example: Extracting a 200-line function into 5 smaller functions that each do one thing clearly.

definition: The skill of writing clear, specific instructions to get better output from AI coding tools. Garbage in, garbage out.
example: Instead of 'make a login', write 'Create a Next.js login page with email/password form, Supabase auth, error handling, and redirect to /dashboard on success.'

definition: When AI generates code that looks correct but uses APIs, functions, or patterns that don't actually exist. Always verify AI output.
example: AI might suggest `import { useAuth } from 'next-auth/hooks'` when that import path doesn't exist in the library.

definition: The folder you're currently in on the command line. Commands you run affect files in this directory.
example: `cd my-project` changes your working directory to the my-project folder.

definition: A message sent from a client (browser) to a server asking for something. Has a method (GET, POST, etc.), URL, headers, and optional body.
example: When you load a webpage, your browser sends a GET request. When you submit a form, it sends a POST request.

definition: JavaScript Object Notation. A text format for sending structured data between systems. The universal language of APIs.
example: `{"name": "Alice", "age": 30}` is JSON. APIs send and receive data in this format.

definition: Verifying WHO someone is. 'Prove you are who you say you are.' Usually done with passwords, tokens, or social login.
example: Logging in with your email and password is authentication. The system checks if your credentials match.

definition: Determining WHAT someone is allowed to do. 'You're logged in, but can you access this resource?'
example: A regular user can edit their own profile, but only admins can delete other users' accounts.

definition: The command that turns a regular folder into a Git repository. Creates a hidden .git folder that tracks all changes.
example: Run `git init` inside your project folder to start tracking changes with Git.

definition: A command that shows the history of commits in your repository. Like a changelog of every save point.
example: `git log --oneline` shows a compact list of recent commits with their messages.

definition: A command that shows what changed between two states. Lines added are shown in green, removed in red.
example: `git diff` shows unstaged changes. `git diff --staged` shows what you're about to commit.

definition: A command that undoes a specific commit by creating a NEW commit that reverses those changes. Safe way to undo.
example: `git revert abc1234` undoes the changes from commit abc1234 without erasing history.

definition: Sending your local commits to a remote repository (like GitHub). Sharing your work with the team.
example: `git push origin main` sends your commits on the main branch to the remote.

definition: Downloading and merging changes from a remote repository into your local copy. Getting the latest work from your team.
example: `git pull origin main` fetches the latest changes and merges them into your local main branch.

definition: Cleaning user input to remove potentially dangerous content before using it. Essential to prevent XSS and injection attacks.
example: Converting `<script>` tags in user comments to plain text so they can't execute as code.

definition: The primary branch of your repository, usually called 'main' or 'master'. Represents the current stable version of your code.
example: Feature branches are created from main and merged back into main when ready.

definition: An error that interrupts the normal flow of your program. If not caught, it crashes your app.
example: Trying to access a property on `null` throws a TypeError exception.

definition: The process of finding and fixing problems in code. Named after literally removing a moth from a computer in the 1940s.
example: Reading the error message, adding console.log statements, checking variable values — that's debugging.

definition: Shortcuts taken in code that save time now but cost more time later. Like credit card debt — small payments add up.
example: Copy-pasting the same logic in 5 places instead of making a reusable function. Works now, nightmare to maintain later.
